coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots/Ag nanoclusters-based fluorescent probe for ratiometric and visual detection of Cu2+
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Lingling
Li, Jiaming
Cai, Hongxin
Shao, Yixin
Zhang, Guoxiang
Chen, Liang
Wang, Yongqiang
Zong, Haitao
Yin, Yuehong
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescent probe, N-doped carbon dots/Ag nanoclusters (N-CDs/AgNCs), was designed and synthesized successfully based on fluorescence inner filler effect (IFE) for the ratiometric and visual detection of Cu2+. Due to the IFE, the as-prepared probe exhibited a weak blue emission at 455 nm and a strong red emission and 665 nm. After adding Cu2+, the red fluorescence can be quenched because of the strong chelating interaction between GSH and Cu2+, whereas the blue fluorescence could be recovered because of the weakening of the IFE, accompanying by a remarkable fluorescence color change from pink to blue under a UV light. Under optimized experimental conditions, the N-CDs/AgNCs probe showed excellent selectivity to Cu2+ and a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity proportion (I665/I455) and Cu2+ concentration in the range of 0–60 μM with a detection limit of 0.13 µM. Further, N-CDs/AgNCs could be used for detecting Cu2+ in tap water and lake water. These results showed that the IFE ratiometric fluorescent probe may provide a new idea for the visualization and semiquantitative detection of Cu2+ and imply that it has great potential to detect Cu2+ in real environment water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent probe
Inner filler effect (IFE)
Cu2+
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs)
Silver nanoclusters (AgNCs)

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties of N-CDs/AgNCs and IFE between N-CDs and AgNCs
Principle and feasibility of N-CDs/AgNCs for the fluorescence detection of Cu2+
Ratiometric fluorescence response of N-CDs/AgNCs to Cu2+
Detection of Cu2+ in real water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of N-CDs/AgNCs. Red circles represent N-CDs, while yellow circles represent AgNCs. The insets above are the grain size distributions of AgNCs and N-CDs. The inset below is HR-TEM image of a single carbon dot. (b) FT-IR spectra of GSH, AgNCs, N-CDs, and N-CDs/AgNCs. (c) XPS spectra of AgNCs. (d) XPS high-resolution spectra of Ag 3d.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of AgNCs, N-CDs and N-CDs/AgNCs. Inset: the photographs of N-CDs and AgNCs under a UV lamp. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs and UV–Vis absorption spectra of AgNCs. (c)Time-resolved decay curves of N-CDs after adding 100–1000 µL AgNCs. (d) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs/AgNCs with different molar ratios. Inset: the photographs of N-CDs/AgNCs under UV light.

(a) The fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs/AgNCs without and within Cu2+. (b) Fluorescence decay curves of N-CDs/AgNCs in the absence and presence of Cu2+. (c) TEM images of N-CDs/AgNCs after the addition of Cu2+. (d) The UV–Vis absorption spectra of N-CDs/AgNCs in the absence and presence of Cu2+ and the fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs.
Optimization of the synthesis conditions. (a) Storage time; (b) UV irradiation time. Optimization of the detection conditions. (c) Reaction time; (d) pH value.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the fluorescent probe with the addition of Cu2+ (0 −60 μM). Inset: the corresponding color change under a UV lamp. (b) Relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I455/I665) and the concentrations of Cu2+.
Selectivity of N-CDs/AgNCs for Cu2+. (a) The changes of I665/I455 value of the probe in the presence of metal ions. (b) The corresponding photographs under a UV lamp.
Schematic presentation of detection of Cu2+ by the IFE-based fluorescent probe.
Comparison of properties of different Cu2+ fluorescent probes.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots/Ag nanoclusters-based fluorescent probe for ratiometric and visual detection of Cu2+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Electronic Information Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo 454000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Electronic Information Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Electronic Information Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, 454000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent probe, N-doped carbon dots/Ag nanoclusters (N-CDs/AgNCs), was designed and synthesized successfully based on fluorescence inner filler effect (IFE) for the ratiometric and visual detection of Cu2+. Due to the IFE, the as-prepared probe exhibited a weak blue emission at 455 nm and a strong red emission and 665 nm. After adding Cu2+, the red fluorescence can be quenched because of the strong chelating interaction between GSH and Cu2+, whereas the blue fluorescence could be recovered because of the weakening of the IFE, accompanying by a remarkable fluorescence color change from pink to blue under a UV light. Under optimized experimental conditions, the N-CDs/AgNCs probe showed excellent selectivity to Cu2+ and a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity proportion (I665/I455) and Cu2+ concentration in the range of 0–60 μM with a detection limit of 0.13 µM. Further, N-CDs/AgNCs could be used for detecting Cu2+ in tap water and lake water. These results showed that the IFE ratiometric fluorescent probe may provide a new idea for the visualization and semiquantitative detection of Cu2+ and imply that it has great potential to detect Cu2+ in real environment water samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper (Cu), as a fundamental trace element in human body and other biological systems, plays a significant role in biochemical reactions and metabolic processes [1,2]. However, it has been proved that the significant fluctuation of Cu2+ level usually brings many serious diseases. For instance, the lack of Cu2+ would cause anemia and the excess of Cu2+ would affect the kidney and liver and cause Alzheimer’s, Wilson, and Menkes diseases [3–5]. In order to keep the body healthy, the copper limits are 15.7–23.6 μM in human blood and 20 μM (1.3 ppm) in the drinking water according to the regulations of the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency. In addition, Cu2+ is also a common metal pollutant in the fishery industry. Therefore, it is very valuable to exploit simple and high sensitivity probe for the detection of Cu2+.

Recently, many techniques have been developed to detect Cu2+ [6–12]. Among these methods, the fluorescent technique has received considerable attention with the advantages of easy operation, fast response, high sensitivity and favorable selectivity. However, most of them are single-wavelength emission fluorescent probes that may be susceptible to the test environment. In order to overcome these problems, the ratiometric fluorescent probe with multiple fluorescence emissions was proposed. Through analyzing the fluorescence intensity ratio at two or more emission wavelengths, it has more reliable and higher sensitive fluorescence signals. More importantly, it can also offer a well visual detecting platform [10]. Accordingly, the design of a ratiometric fluorescent probe is of great significance for high sensitive and visual monitoring Cu2+.

As a highlighted fluorescent probe, carbon dots (CDs) possess a series of advantages including simple preparation, low toxicity, ease of functionalization, great biocompatibility, and fluorescence characteristics, are considered as a promising functional nanomaterial in chemical and biological sensing, photocatalysis, energy storage, etc [12–17]. However, the relatively low quantum yield of CDs synthesized from a single carbon source hindered their widespread use. To enhance the quantum yields and optical properties, heteroatom doping (nitrogen, phosphorus, sulfur, and boron, etc.) was adopted [18]. Since the atomic size of nitrogen atom is similar to that of carbon atom and the nitrogen atom can easily inject electrons into CDs, nitrogen doping is the most widely used way to improve the fluorescence properties. Nitrogen doping can produce new surface state energy levels and facilitate radiative recombination. In addition, the electron-donating amino-groups can enhance the conjugation extent, accelerate the electron transitions from the ground state to the lowest excited singlet state, and then increase the fluorescence quantum yield. Nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) show excellent performances in the field of fluorescence sensing and environmental monitoring. Metal nanoclusters (NCs), due to the unique electronic structures, strong luminescence and excellent photostability, make them ideal fluorescent probes and also have attracted more and more attentions [19–23]. Because of low-cost and easy synthesis, Ag nanoclusters (AgNCs) have been widely used in fluorescence sensing [24]. Thus, in the work, the nitrogen-doped carbon dots with blue emission and the Ag nanoclusters with red emission were chosen as two different fluorescence materials to construct a ratiometric fluorescent probe. During the process of construction, several different sensing mechanisms may be employed, such as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), aggregation-induced emission (AIE), and inner filter effect (IFE). Among them, the IFE-based probe is more flexible and straightforward without any restricted link between the energy donor and acceptor, and the efficiency is only dependent on the spectral overlap extent [25].

Herein, we reported an IFE-based ratiometric fluorescent probe (N-CDs/AgNCs) for the detection of Cu2+ ( Scheme 1). Firstly, the blue fluorescence of N-CDs was quenched by AgNCs through the IFE. Then, the addition of Cu2+ could markedly quench the red fluorescence of AgNCs passivated by GSH via the GSH-Cu2+ interaction, weaken the IFE, and recover the blue fluorescence of N-CDs. Accordingly, the proportion of two emission peaks gradually changes with the concentration of Cu2+, and the corresponding quantitative relationship could be established. Moreover, the probe could realize visual analysis of Cu2+ because it is easier to observe with the naked eye under UV light. In this way, a convenient and visual detection technique for detecting Cu2+ was provided based on N-CDs/AgNCs.

α-lipoic acid (LA), L-Glutathione (GSH), copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)2), and silver nitrate (AgNO3) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Each metal salt was provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were obtained from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All of the chemicals were of analytical grade and used directly without further purification. All glass containers were cleaned with aqua regia and ultrapure water before the experiments. Ultrapure water was used in the whole experiment.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was carried out to characterize the size and morphology of N-CDs/AgNCs by the Tecnai G2 F30 (FEI, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained from a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS5 FT-IR spectroscopy (ThermoFisher, USA). UV-Vis absorption spectra were collected with a UV-3600 spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The optical photographs under the UV lamp were taken by a smartphone (Huawei). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi spectrometer (ThermoFisher, USA). Fluorescence emission spectra were measured on a Horiba Fluorolog-3 C-22 spectrofluorometer (Horiba, USA). The fluorescence lifetime was recorded by using the time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) technique.

N-CDs were prepared by the following steps [26]. (1) Dissolve 4.2 g citric acid and 3.6 g urea into 40 mL ultrapure water and sonicate for 20 min (2) Transfer the above solution to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heat the autoclave at 180 ºC for 4 h, and then cool down the autoclave naturally to room temperature. (3) Filter the dark-green solution using 0.22 µm membranes and purify by dialysis (1000 Da) for 24 h. The as-prepared N-CDs solution was stored at 4 °C in the refrigerator.

AgNCs were synthesized according to the previous method with minor modification [27]. Specifically, 125 µL AgNO3 (20 mM) and 150 µL GSH (50 mM) aqueous solution were added into 5 mL ultrapure water under gently stirring. Then, the pH value was adjusted to 9 by using 1 M NaOH. Meanwhile, 5 mg LA powder was mixed with 10 µL freshly prepared NaBH4 (0.6 M) and the solution was stirring for 30 min until it became clear. During this process, insoluble LA was transformed into soluble dihydrolipoic acid (DHLA) and the solution became clear. Subsequently, the DHLA solution was immediately added into the silver mixture by rapidly stirring for 1 min. Thereafter, a slightly excessive NaBH4 solution was slowly dripped into the above mixture and under vigorous stirring for 20 min. After 1.5 h incubation at room temperature, the mixture was further dialyzed and purified. Afterwards, the AgNCs solution was conserved within a 4 °C refrigerator for later experiments.

The N-CDs/AgNCs ratiometric fluorescence probe was constructed via a coupling technique. Briefly, 100 µL N-CDs were mixed with appropriate AgNCs. Then the mixture was added into 2 mL PBS buffer. After stirring vigorously, the solution was incubated for 10 min at room temperature. Finally, the prepared N-CDs/AgNCs solution was stored at 4 °C for further experiments. To achieve optimum performance, the ratio of N-CDs and AgNCs was selected as 10:1. Under this ratio, a distinct luminescence change from pink to blue can be observed.

The N-CDs/AgNCs probe was prepared by mixing 100 µL N-CDs and 1 mL AgNCs. Different amounts of Cu2+ standard solutions were introduced to the probe solution, and the final concentrations of Cu2+ were maintained 0–60 μM, respectively. After stirring well and incubating for 5 min, the fluorescence spectra of mixed solutions were collected on a fluorescence spectrometer from 400 to 800 nm at the 370 nm excitation wavelength and the color changes was observed under a UV lamp. All the measurements were measured three times. The selectivity of the N-CDs/AgNCs probe was evaluated by investigating the fluorescent properties of the probe toward various metal ions (Zn2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Co2 +, Al3+, Na+, Ca2+, and K+) under the same conditions. In addition, the potential application of the probe prepared to detect Cu2+ in real samples was also studied.

The morphology and structure of N-CDs/AgNCs were investigated by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1(a), the red circle represented N-CDs with a higher contrast, and the yellow circle represented AgNCs with lighter contrast. Obviously, both N-CDs and AgNCs were uniform and monodispersed and these particles are almost round shaped with average size of ∼3.25 nm and 2.78 nm (the insets above in Fig. 1(a)), respectively. No large Ag particles were observed, indicating that bright red luminescent materials are composed of small AgNCs. The HR-TEM image of N-CDs (the inset below in Fig. 1(a)) demonstrated the lattice spacing between the adjacent planes was 0.24 nm and considerably smaller than the interlayer distance of 0.34–0.36 nm between graphene layers in graphite [2]. The observation suggests the nanodot is not graphitic carbon. In order to obtain a better insight of the surface functional groups of the N-CDs, FT-IR analysis was performed. As displayed in Fig. 1(b), the blue curve was the FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs. Absorption bands at 3100–3500 cm−1 corresponded to the amine and hydroxyl groups [28]. The two characteristic bands at 1560 cm−1 and 1401 cm−1 were assigned to the CO and C-N stretching vibrations, respectively [28,29]. The absorption band at 1164 cm−1 was ascribed to the bending vibrations of C-O-C [28]. All these results revealed that N has been successfully doped in to the carbon dots and the N-CDs were rich in carbonyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups on their surfaces. These hydrophilic functional groups would improve the stability and hydrophilicity of N-CDs, and then widen their application in an aqueous system.

To clarify the surface composition of AgNCs and the chemical state of Ag element, XPS spectra were employed. In Fig. 1(c), the full spectrum shows five peaks, corresponding to C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, S 2p and Ag 3d, and the corresponding binding energy appeared at 284.89, 399.86, 531.78, 163.51, and 367.99 eV, respectively. XPS results indicate that the surface of AgNCs is mainly composed of C, N, O, Ag and S elements which may come from the GSH protecting agent and Ag precursor. The spectrum of C 1 s can be divided into three peaks, corresponding to the functional groups of C–C/CC (284.8 eV), C-O/C-N (286.5 eV), and CO (288.3 eV) (Fig. S1(a)). In the N 1 s and O 1 s high-resolution XPS (Fig. S1(b) and (c)), the peaks of 399.8 eV and 531.8 eV mainly correspond to the C-N and C-O/CO bonds. In the Ag 3d high-resolution (Fig. 1(d)), the two peaks of 368.0 eV for Ag 3d5/2 and 374.0 eV for Ag 3d3/2 could be assigned to the presence of Ag0 [30]. It's worth noting that the binding energy for Ag 3d5/2 of Ag0 is 368.0 eV. Compared with the reference value (Ag0: 368.2 eV) [31,32], a distinct shift towards lower binding energy, implying the existence of the interaction between AgNCs and GSH. In order to confirm the existence of the interaction, the FT-IR spectra of AgNCs (red curve) and GSH (black curve) were measured, as shown in Fig. 1(b). Compared the two FT-IR spectra carefully, it can be seen clearly that the 2524 cm−1 band of S–H was only found in pure GSH and has disappeared in GSH-stabilized AgNCs. Such observation suggested that during the forming process of GSH-stabilized AgNCs, the S-H bond would be opened and the H would be replaced by Ag to form the stronger Ag-S band because of the good affinity between Ag and S–H of GSH. In this way, the GSH has been modified successfully onto the surface of AgNCs. The result is in agreement with the XPS spectra. Due to the formation of Ag-S between AgNCs and GSH, oxygen atoms have high electronegativity near the silver atoms, reducing their electronic density, leading to the movement of binding energy. This interaction is beneficial for keeping the dispersibility of AgNCs and enhancing the stability of AgNCs. Moreover, those characteristic peaks observed at 3350 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1 suggested the presence of amine groups and carboxyl groups in AgNCs, indicating that the synthesized silver nanoclusters have good water solubility.

Besides, the FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs/AgNCs (green curve) was also measured to characterize the surface composition of N-CDs/AgNCs probe. As shown in the Fig. 1(b), the absorption peaks at 1560 and 1401 cm−1 of N-CDs and 3400 cm−1 of AgNCs were also observed in the spectrum of the N-CDs/AgNCs, which indicated that the N-CDs/AgNCs probe was prepared successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of N-CDs/AgNCs and IFE between N-CDs and AgNCs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to clearly display the optical properties of the N-CDs/AgNCs probe, the fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs, AgNCs and N-CDs/AgNCs were investigated, as shown in Fig. 2(a). Under 370 nm excitation, AgNCs have a weaker red emission peak at 665 nm, while N-CDs have a stronger blue emission peak at 455 nm. The large difference between the two emission peaks was very conducive to the visual detection of Cu2+. The inset showed the photos of N-CDs and AgNCs under a UV lamp. Clearly, N-CDs and AgNCs solutions could emit bright blue and red fluorescence, respectively. Based on the XPS and FT-IR results above, the red emission of AgNCs could be attributed to the electronic transition between the sulfur atom and the Ag0 nucleus, resulting in the metal centered photoluminescence [33]. The blue emission of N-CDs could be ascribed to the high yield of radiative recombination from the nitrogen-doped surface states [26,34]. However, after mixing AgNCs with N-CDs, the red fluorescence of AgNCs have no change and the blue fluorescence of N-CDs decreased significantly, which indicated that AgNCs could quench the fluorescence of N-CDs.

To explore the principle of N-CDs quenching by AgNCs, the UV-Vis absorption spectra of N-CDs and the fluorescence spectra of AgNCs were measured. As seen from Fig. 2(b), the absorption band of AgNCs is 400–550 nm and the emission spectrum of N-CDs overlapped with the UV-Vis absorption spectrum of AgNCs to a great extent, implying that the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by AgNCs might be caused by the IFE or the FRET. Previous research has shown that the FRET process would reduce the fluorescence lifetime of the donor, namely N-CDs, while the fluorescence lifetime of the donor would have almost no change during the IFE process. Therefore, it is possible to distinguish between IFE and FRET by studying the variation of the fluorescence lifetime of the donor. Different concentrations of AgNCs were mixed with N-CDs solutions to record fluorescence lifetime. Fig. 2(c) showed the changes in the lifetime of N-CDs after adding distinct concentrations of AgNCs (0, 200, 400, 600, 800 and 1000 µL). The average fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs was 1.56, 1.54, 1.55, 1.54, 1.57 and 1.59 ns by fitting the double exponential decay function. Obviously, the fluorescence lifetime only has a little change, indicating that the quenching process is static quenching. That is to say, the cause of fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by AgNCs is IFE rather than the FRET. As a result, it is viable to choose N-CDs/AgNCs as donor–acceptor pairs to fabricate IFE ratiometric fluorescent probe.

As seen from Fig. 2(d), the fluorescence of N-CDs (100 µL) weakened rapidly and the fluorescence of AgNCs increased gradually as the AgNCs addition amount increased. Meanwhile, a distinct luminescence change from blue to pink can be observed. When 1000 µL AgNCs were added, the emission spectra of AgNCs and N-CDs present appropriate emission intensity and good peak shapes. Therefore, to meet the visual detection require, the N-CDs/AgNCs with 1000 µL AgNCs and 100 µL N-CDs was chosen as the fluorescent probe for the detection of Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle and feasibility of N-CDs/AgNCs for the fluorescence detection of Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: After adding Cu2+ to N-CDs/AgNCs, Cu2+ reacted with the AgNCs and N-CDs respectively. As seen from Fig. 3(a), the fluorescence of AgNCs was quenched and the fluorescence of N-CDs was enhanced, accompanying by the variations in fluorescence intensity and color. Fig. S2 depicted the effects of Cu2+ on the fluorescence of AgNCs and N-CDs, respectively. As the concentration of Cu2+ increased, the fluorescence of AgNCs greatly decreased whereas the fluorescence of N-CDs was slightly affected, which indicated that AgNCs and N-CDs could be act as a response signal and background signal in the detection of Cu2+, respectively.

The fluorescence quenching of N-CDs/AgNCs is generally ascribed by dynamic or static quenching. To illustrate the mechanism of Cu2+-induced fluorescence quenching process, the fluorescence lifetime with and without the addition of Cu2+ were investigated, as shown in Fig. 3(b). Through fitting the data, the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs/AgNCs without and with Cu2+ was determined to be 1.53 ns and 1.54 ns, respectively. The little change in fluorescence lifetime revealed that static quenching is predominant and dynamic quenching could be ruled out. The result implied that Cu2+ may react with AgNCs to form a non-fluorescent complex and then quench the fluorescence of AgNCs. In addition, it has been demonstrated that GSH has the strong chelating ability or affinity to Cu2+ [27]. In other words, Cu2+ could chelate with the carboxyl and amine groups [35] on surface of N-CDs/AgNCs in molar ratio 1:2 to form a stable tetragonal Cu2+-GSH complex, block the charge transfer of ligand to Ag, cause the aggregation of N-CDs/AgNCs, and thereby result in the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs/AgNCs. The Cu2+ induced aggregation was shown in Fig. 3(c). As shown in Fig. 3(c), the dispersed N-CDs/AgNCs agglomerated significantly after adding Cu2+ because of the formation of Cu2+-GSH complexes. Furthermore, the absorption intensity of N-CDs/AgNCs at 300–550 nm was greatly changed. These observations confirmed the formation of non-fluorescent complexes. The decrease of the absorption intensity at 400–550 nm would result in the disappearance of spectral overlap between the absorption of AgNCs and emission of N-CDs, weakening the IFE process between AgNCs and N-CDs, and thereby recovering the fluorescence of N-CDs. Therefore, after adding Cu2+ to N-CDs/AgNCs, the fluorescence of AgNCs was quenched and the fluorescence of CDs was recovered. The above results showed that the ratiometric fluorescent probe N-CDs/AgNCs is feasible for detecting Cu2+.

The experimental conditions would greatly affect the detection performances. Therefore, the synthesis conditions and detection conditions, including storage time, ultraviolet light irradiation time, reaction time and pH value, have been carefully optimized before the detection of Cu2+. To be specific, the pH value of the solution was tuned in the range of 3.0–10.0 by adjusting the ratio of HCl to NaOH. 10 μM Cu2+ was selected as the test object and the intensity ratio (I665/I455) was chosen as the detection response value. I665 and I455 is the fluorescence intensity of AgNCs and N-CDs in the probe.

Fig. 4(a) and (b) showed the influences of storage time and UV irradiation time on the ratio of I665/I455. As shown in Fig. 4(b), the intensity ratio of the freshly prepared probe will gradually increase with time within 24 h, and reach the maximum after one day. Further increasing the storage time, the ratio no longer increased, indicating that the synthesized N-CDs/AgNCs probe has good stability. Therefore, N-CDs/AgNCs stored for 26 h were selected to construct the probe. From Fig. 4(b), it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity ratio was little affected by the UV irradiation time, further implying the good stability of the probe.

The effect of detection conditions on the fluorescence of the N-CDs/AgNCs probe was also studied. As seen from Fig. 4(c), the relationship between the intensity ratio (I665/I455) and the reaction time was illustrated. The ratio increased rapidly to a maximum within 1 min after adding Cu2+, and then remained stable, indicating that the probe can recognize Cu2+ quickly. A fast response is necessary for ion detection. Thus, to ensure the reaction was completed, a reaction time of 3 min was adopted for further experiments. The effect of the pH value on the ratio was investigated as shown in Fig. 4(d). The result showed that the pH has a remarkable influence on the intensity ratio. When pH was 9, the fluorescence change Δ(I665/I455) of the probe reached the maximum. PH 9 was close to the real test environment. Therefore, we chose 9 as the optimum pH for the detection of Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence response of N-CDs/AgNCs to Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal experimental conditions, the N-CDs/AgNCs probe was applied to detect Cu2+ and the results were displayed in Fig. 5(a). Under 370 nm excitation, the N-CDs/AgNCs probe emitted two fluorescence peaks located at 455 nm and 665 nm, respectively. As Cu2+ concentration increased from 0–60 µM, the fluorescence intensity at 665 nm decreased gradually and the fluorescence intensity at 455 nm increased continuously, respectively. As seen from Fig. 5(b), the fluorescence intensity proportion (I455/I665) and the concentration of Cu2+ ranging from 1 to 60 μM showed a good linear relationship. The regression equation is Y= 0.0215X-0.1313 (R2 =0.9915). The limit of detection (LOD) for Cu2+ was calculated to be 0.13 μM by the detection limit formula 3σ/S (σ is the standard deviation of 20 blank samples, and S is the slope of the linear equation), which is lower than the maximum Cu2+ in drinking water set by the World Health Organization and the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) of 2 mg/ mL and 1.3 mg/mL. As shown in Table 1, the detection limit of this probe was lower than most of the previous fluorescent probe and then most of the probe only have single emission, which would significantly affect the detection sensitive. Furthermore, after adding Cu2+, a distinguishable fluorescence color transformed from pink to blue under the UV lamp (Inset of Fig. 5(a)), which implied that N-CDs/AgNCs could be used as a visual probe for semi-quantitative detection of Cu2+. Accordingly, the N-CDs/AgNCs was proposed as a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe for the detection of Cu2+.

The anti-interference capacity to Cu2+ detection was evaluated. Under the same experimental conditions, 10 μM Cu2+ and 100 μM interfering metal ions (Zn2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Co2+, Al3+, Na+, Ca2+, and K+) commonly found in the environment was incubated with N-CDs/AgNCs for 15 min, and then the fluorescence spectra were measured. As shown in Fig. 6(a), the fluorescence intensity ratio (I665/I455) was quenched about 80% by Cu2+ at 10 μM, while no obvious change in the color and fluorescence intensity was detected after adding individual interfering metal ion at 100 μM into the probe solution. The corresponding photographs under a UV lamp were taken (Fig. 6(b)). Obviously, the color changed only when Cu2+ was added. The fluorescence quenching of GSH-stabilized AgNCs by metal ions can be attributed to the chelating interaction between GSH and metal ions, depending on the binding energies and the amount of charge transfer between metal ions with GSH. Because the binding energies of alkali metals ions (K+ and Na+), alkali earth metals ions (Mg2+ and Ca2+), Pb2+, Al3+ and GSH are relatively low, they have the weak fluorescence quenching abilities. During the interaction between GSH and transition metal ions (Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, and Co2+), the amount of charge transfer was small and the interaction energy was low, hence, the fluorescence quenching abilities was also weak. Due to the strong chelating ability between GSH and Cu2+, GSH of GSH-stabilized AgNCs may chelate with Cu2+ in molar ratio 2:1 to form a stable tetragonal geometry of Cu2+-GSH complex, block the charge transfer of ligand to Ag, cause the aggregation of N-CDs/AgNCs, and thereby result in the strong fluorescence quenching. These data indicated that the N-CDs/AgNCs fluorescent probe has a high selectivity and anti-interference ability for Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the potential application of the Cu2+ probe in real water samples, the performance of the present analysis method was challenged by the tap water and lake water from the Xinyue Lake of Henan Polytechnic University. Before the experiments, the lake water samples were filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane to remove the dust and particles and centrifuged at 8 000 rpm for 10 min. The tap samples were directly measured without any purification. Then, 1 mL of N-CDs/AgNCs was added into 1000 µL of each unknown sample (the mixture solution of the real water and Cu2+ with different addition amount), and after incubating 5 min, the results were recorded, as shown in Fig. S3 and Table S1. The concentration of Cu2+ in the real samples detected by the probe was consistent with the addition concentration. The recoveries of Cu2+ were in the range of 96.75–101.83% and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were less than 4.96%. This revealed that the N-CDs/AgNCs probe possesses good accuracy and repeatability, and it has great potential to detect Cu2+ in environmental water.

In conclusion, we successfully synthesized an IFE ratiometric fluorescent probe N-CDs/AgNCs with dual-emission peaks at 455 and 665 nm. The probe can be used for the detection of Cu2+ in the range of 1–60 μM with a detection limit of 0.13 µM. When different amount of Cu2+ were introduced into the probe, the red fluorescence of AgNCs was quenched rapidly caused by the specific aggregation introduced by the strong chelating interaction between Cu2+ and GSH, whereas, the blue fluorescence of N-CDs was recovered gradually because of the reduction of the UV-Vis absorption peak of AgNCs and the weakening of the IFE between AgNCs and N-CDs, which can realize the visual detection of Cu2+. The probe showed good sensitivity and selectivity to Cu2+. In addition, the probe could be used for detecting Cu2+ in tap water and lake water, which proved the probe has great application prospect in detecting Cu2+ in real environment water.

Yan Lingling: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Li Jiaming: Data curation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Cai Hongxin: Resources, Visualization, Project administration. Shao Yixin: Investigation, Validation. Zhang Guoxiang: Writing – original draft, Validation. Chen Liang: Data curation, Visualization. Wang Yongqiang: Writing – review & editing. Zong Haitao: Funding acquisition. Yin Yuehong: Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant number 61705062], the Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province [grant number 212300410348], and the Henan Province Key Research and Development and Promotion Special [grant numbers 202102310228 and 212102310099].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2023.169227.

ce:section.ce:para:
